description: Common Finnish adverbs for describing how, when, and where

items.notes: Pronounced 'NO-pe-as-ti'. Formed from adjective 'nopea' (fast) + -sti suffix, similar to English '-ly'.
examples: Hän juoksee nopeasti. — He/she runs quickly.
Tule nopeasti! — Come quickly!

items.notes: Pronounced 'HI-taas-ti'. From adjective 'hidas' (slow) + -sti suffix. Double 'a' is long.
examples: Puhu hitaasti, kiitos. — Speak slowly, please.
Auto ajoi hitaasti. — The car drove slowly.

items.notes: Pronounced 'HY-vin'. Extremely common. Means 'well' (manner) and also 'very' (degree). From 'hyvä' (good).
examples: Puhut suomea hyvin. — You speak Finnish well.
Olen hyvin väsynyt. — I am very tired.

items.notes: Pronounced 'HUO-nos-ti'. From adjective 'huono' (bad) + -sti suffix. The 'uo' is a diphthong.
examples: Nukuin huonosti. — I slept badly.
Koe meni huonosti. — The exam went poorly.

items.notes: Pronounced 'PAL-jon'. Very common word. Used with partitive case: paljon vettä (a lot of water).
examples: Kiitos paljon! — Thank you very much!
Syön paljon hedelmiä. — I eat a lot of fruits.

items.notes: Pronounced 'VÄ-hän'. Both vowels are front vowels (ä). Used with partitive: vähän maitoa (a little milk).
examples: Puhun vähän suomea. — I speak a little Finnish.
Odota vähän. — Wait a little.

items.notes: Pronounced 'AI-na'. The 'ai' diphthong. Short and very common word.
examples: Hän on aina myöhässä. — He/she is always late.
Juon aina kahvia aamulla. — I always drink coffee in the morning.

items.notes: Pronounced 'U-sein'. The 'ei' diphthong sounds like 'ay' in 'say'.
examples: Käyn usein kirjastossa. — I often visit the library.
Syömme usein ulkona. — We often eat out.

items.notes: Pronounced 'JOS-kus'. From 'jos' (if) + -kus. Used for occasional events.
examples: Joskus sataa. — Sometimes it rains.
Käyn joskus uimassa. — I sometimes go swimming.

items.notes: Pronounced 'HAR-voin'. From 'harva' (sparse/rare). The 'oi' is a diphthong.
examples: Syön harvoin lihaa. — I rarely eat meat.
Hän tulee harvoin myöhään. — He/she rarely comes late.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KOS-kaan'. Double 'a' is long. Used with 'ei' for 'never': en koskaan = I never. Without negation, means 'ever'.
examples: En ole koskaan käynyt Suomessa. — I have never been to Finland.
Oletko koskaan maistanut salmiakkia? — Have you ever tasted salmiak?

items.notes: Pronounced 'YO'. Very short word, just one syllable. Extremely common in everyday speech.
examples: Olen jo syönyt. — I have already eaten.
Onko hän jo täällä? — Is he/she already here?

items.notes: Pronounced 'VIE-lä'. The 'ie' diphthong. Means 'still' in positive sentences, 'yet' in questions and negatives.
examples: Hän nukkuu vielä. — He/she is still sleeping.
Ei vielä. — Not yet.

items.notes: Pronounced 'NYT'. Short one-syllable word. The 'y' sounds like German 'ü'. Very common.
examples: Tule tänne nyt! — Come here now!
Mitä teemme nyt? — What do we do now?

items.notes: Pronounced 'SIT-ten'. Double 't' is long. Used for sequence ('then') and past time ('ago'): kaksi vuotta sitten = two years ago.
examples: Ensin syömme, sitten lähdemme. — First we eat, then we leave.
Kaksi päivää sitten. — Two days ago.

items.notes: Pronounced 'TÄÄL-lä'. Double 'ä' and double 'l'. Means 'here' as a location. Compare: 'tänne' = 'to here' (direction).
examples: Istun täällä. — I'm sitting here.
Onko kukaan täällä? — Is anyone here?

items.notes: Pronounced 'SIEL-lä'. Double 'l'. Means 'there' as a location. Compare: 'sinne' = 'to there' (direction).
examples: Hän asuu siellä. — He/she lives there.
Mitä siellä tapahtuu? — What's happening there?

items.notes: Pronounced 'YL-lä'. Double 'l' is long. Used as an adverb or postposition with genitive: pöydän yllä = above the table.
examples: Lamppu on yllä. — The lamp is above.
Lintu lensi meidän yllämme. — A bird flew above us.

items.notes: Pronounced 'AL-la'. Double 'l' is long. Used as adverb or postposition with genitive: pöydän alla = under the table.
examples: Kissa on pöydän alla. — The cat is under the table.
Laita se tämän alle. — Put it under this.

items.notes: Pronounced 'E-des-sä'. Double 's' is long. Also a postposition with genitive: talon edessä = in front of the house.
examples: Auto on talon edessä. — The car is in front of the house.
Istun sinun edessäsi. — I sit in front of you.

items.notes: Pronounced 'TA-ka-na'. Also a postposition with genitive: talon takana = behind the house.
examples: Puisto on talon takana. — The park is behind the house.
Kuka on oven takana? — Who is behind the door?

items.notes: Pronounced 'LÄ-hel-lä'. Double 'l' is long. Also a postposition with partitive: koulua lähellä = near the school.
examples: Kauppa on lähellä. — The shop is nearby.
Asun lähellä keskustaa. — I live near the center.

items.notes: Pronounced 'KAU-ka-na'. The 'au' diphthong. Opposite of 'lähellä'. Also postposition with elative: kaupungista kaukana = far from the city.
examples: Lentokenttä on kaukana. — The airport is far away.
Hän asuu kaukana täältä. — He/she lives far from here.

items.notes: Pronounced 'YH-des-sä'. From 'yksi' (one). Double 's' is long. A warm, commonly used word.
examples: Teemme työtä yhdessä. — We work together.
Syödään yhdessä. — Let's eat together.

items.notes: Pronounced 'E-rik-seen'. Double 'e' at the end is long. Opposite of 'yhdessä'.
examples: Maksamme erikseen. — We pay separately.
Pidä nämä erikseen. — Keep these separate.
